value.content:
Two repricings hit crypto allocation in 2026: DeepSeek's permanent inference price cuts, and BofA Hartnett calling it "the biggest bubble since the railway bubble."

Here's what serious capital is actually doing. 🧵

What allocators want in 2026 — tokenized RWAs, regulated stablecoins, institutional execution venues, and the AI-compute + energy stack.

Narratives are out. Substance is in.

DeepSeek's price-cut cycle forces every $NVDA bull to re-underwrite the compute layer.

Capital that used to chase L1 narratives now sits on data-center and energy theses — with a crypto settlement tail.

Hartnett's "biggest bubble since the railway bubble" isn't a sell call. He wants two specific signals first.

Allocators are pricing that posture in: exposure, but with downside structure. Tokenized credit beats directional spot in that frame.

Where capital still gets stuck — 4 chokepoints:

• Custody — counterparty cannot be audited
• Mandate — no crypto allocation bucket exists
• Due diligence — strategy not documented
• Sizing — no real risk model → defaults to zero

The underrated blocker: banking.

Try opening a corp account in HK or Frankfurt and saying you'll wire to a digital-asset venue. You get rejected even when every leg is licensed.

Regulated stablecoins like $USDC are quietly solving this faster than anyone expected.

The most expensive blocker is reputational, inside the institution.

Every allocation passes a committee. Someone has to stake their name. Until that person is comfortable being first, capital stays in cash.

Deals don't close — they get pushed to next quarter.

Where demand goes next:

• Tokenized private credit
• Regulated stablecoins as settlement rail
• Institutional prime brokerage
• AI-compute crossover

Boring instruments doing high volume. That's what mature capital looks like.

Trust is the new gold.

Substance, regulated venues, documented strategies, survivability through stress.

Everything else is railway-company stock with a different name.
